question_text: Let $A = { \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] }$ and $I _ { 3 }$ be the $\mathbf { 3 \times 3 }$ identity matrix. Then the nullity of $5 A ( I _ { 3 } + A + \overline { { { A } } } ^ { 2 } )$ equals
solution:
Step 1: Compute $I _ { 3 } + A + A ^ { 2 }$

We begin by calculating the powers of matrix A:

 $ A ^ { 2 } = { \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] } ^ { 2 } = { \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { 1 } & { 0 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] } = I _ { 3 } $ 

Thus,

 $ I _ { 3 } + A + A ^ { 2 } = I _ { 3 } + A + I _ { 3 } = 2 I _ { 3 } + A . $ 

Step 2: Multiply by 5A:

 $ 5 A ( I _ { 3 } + A + A ^ { 2 } ) = 5 A ( 2 I _ { 3 } + A ) = 1 0 A + 5 A ^ { 2 } . $ 

Since $A ^ { 2 } = I _ { 3 }$

 $ 5 A ( I _ { 3 } + A + A ^ { 2 } ) = 1 0 A + 5 I _ { 3 } . $ 

Step 3: Find the nullity. The nullity of a matrix is the dimension of the null space. Since the matrix $1 0 A + 5 I _ { 3 }$ is a linear combination of A and $I _ { 3 }$ , it has a nullity of 1.

Thus, the nullity is 1.
Quick Tip

The nullity of a matrix is the number of free variables in its solution to the homogeneous equation $A \mathbf { x } = 0 ,$
